bibliographic_data.title: Deep Domain Adaptation: A Sim2Real Neural Approach for Improving Eye-Tracking Systems
bibliographic_data.abstract: Eye image segmentation is a critical step in eye tracking that has great influence over the final gaze estimate. Segmentation models trained using supervised machine learning can excel at this task, their effectiveness is determined by the degree of overlap between the narrow distributions of image properties defined by the target dataset and highly specific training datasets, of which there are few. Attempts to broaden the distribution of existing eye image datasets through the inclusion of synthetic eye images have found that a model trained on synthetic images will often fail to generalize back to real-world eye images. In remedy, we use dimensionality-reduction techniques to measure the overlap between the target eye images and synthetic training data, and to prune the training dataset in a manner that maximizes distribution overlap. We demonstrate that our methods result in robust, improved performance when tackling the discrepancy between simulation and real-world data samples.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a method to close the domain gap between synthetic eye images and real-world eye images by leveraging a learned neural distance model, resulting in improved segmentation performance on real-world test datasets.
core_content.key_methodology: Dimensionality-reduction techniques are used to measure the overlap between target eye images and synthetic training data. The training dataset is pruned to maximize distribution overlap, ensuring that the eye-tracking CNN model is trained with a large number of synthetically generated images in proportion to real-world images.
outcomes: Improved performance on real-world test datasets
Higher generalization performance compared to baseline models trained on synthetic images only
core_content.full_text_markdown: Eye image segmentation plays a critical role in eye tracking, significantly impacting the accuracy of gaze estimation. Traditional approaches rely on deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and require large datasets for training. However, real-world datasets are costly to collect due to high labeling burden and privacy issues, while synthetic datasets can be generated more easily but suffer from a 'reality gap' with real-world images. This paper addresses the sim2real problem by proposing a method that uses dimensionality-reduction techniques to measure the overlap between target eye images and synthetic training data, pruning the dataset to maximize distribution overlap. The approach ensures that the eye-tracking CNN model is trained on a balanced mix of synthetic and real-world images, leading to improved performance in real-world scenarios.
